questions.question: Which gas do plants take in to make food during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants use carbon dioxide (CO2) from air, water from soil, and sunlight to make food (glucose) through photosynthesis.

questions.question: What is the speed of light?
questions.explanation: The speed of light in vacuum is approximately 3 × 10^8 m/s (300,000 km/s).

questions.question: Which part of the plant makes food using sunlight?
questions.explanation: Leaves contain chlorophyll (green pigment) and perform photosynthesis to make food using sunlight, CO2, and water.

questions.question: What type of energy does the Sun give to Earth?
questions.explanation: The Sun releases nuclear energy through fusion reactions, which reaches Earth as solar/radiant energy (light and heat).

questions.question: Which vitamin is produced in the body by exposure to sunlight?
questions.explanation: Vitamin D is synthesized in the skin when exposed to ultraviolet B (UVB) radiation from sunlight. It is essential for bone health.

questions.question: Sound cannot travel through:
questions.explanation: Sound requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel. It cannot travel through a vacuum because there are no particles to transmit the vibrations.

questions.question: The phenomenon of light bending when it passes from one medium to another is:
questions.explanation: Refraction is the bending of light when it passes from one transparent medium to another with different optical density (e.g., from air to glass).

questions.question: Which gas is produced during photosynthesis and released into the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Oxygen is released as a byproduct of photosynthesis when water molecules are split (photolysis) to provide electrons for the light reactions.

questions.question: The 'Photoelectric Effect' was explained by:
questions.explanation: Albert Einstein explained the photoelectric effect in 1905 using the concept of light as photons (quanta of energy). This work won him the Nobel Prize in Physics (1921).

questions.question: Which is the lightest element in the periodic table?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen (H) is the lightest element with atomic number 1 and atomic mass ~1. It is also the most abundant element in the universe.

questions.question: The speed of light in vacuum is:
questions.explanation: The speed of light in vacuum (c) is exactly 299,792,458 m/s ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s ≈ 300,000 km/s. Nothing can travel faster than the speed of light (Einstein's special relativity).

questions.question: Which phenomenon causes the rainbow to form?
options: Dispersion and total internal reflection of sunlight in water droplets
questions.explanation: Rainbow forms because sunlight entering a water droplet is refracted, internally reflected, and dispersed (separated into colours). The 7 colours (VIBGYOR — Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red) are separated.

questions.question: Total Internal Reflection occurs when:
options: Light travels from denser to rarer medium at an angle greater than the critical angle
questions.explanation: TIR occurs when light travels from a denser to a rarer medium and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle. TIR is the principle behind optical fibers and diamond's brilliance.

questions.question: A convex lens is used in:
options: Cameras, magnifying glasses, and correcting farsightedness (hypermetropia)
questions.explanation: Convex (converging) lenses are thicker in the middle. They converge parallel light rays to a focal point. Used in cameras, magnifying glasses, telescopes (objective lens), and to correct farsightedness.

questions.question: A concave lens is used to correct:
questions.explanation: Concave (diverging) lenses are thinner in the middle and diverge light rays. They are used to correct myopia (nearsightedness) — where distant objects appear blurry.

questions.question: Which colour of light has the shortest wavelength?
questions.explanation: Violet light has the shortest wavelength (~380-450 nm) and highest frequency in the visible spectrum. Red has the longest wavelength (~620-750 nm) and lowest frequency. The visible spectrum is VIBGYOR.

questions.question: The primary colours of light (additive colour mixing) are:
questions.explanation: Primary colours of light: Red, Green, Blue (RGB — additive mixing). Combining all three gives white light. Computer/TV screens use RGB. Pigment primary colours (subtractive) are Cyan, Magenta, Yellow (CMY).

questions.question: Sound travels fastest in:
questions.explanation: Sound is a mechanical wave (needs a medium). It travels fastest in solids (5,960 m/s in steel), slower in liquids (1,497 m/s in water), and slowest in gases (343 m/s in air at 20°C). It cannot travel in vacuum.

questions.question: The speed of sound in air at 20°C is approximately:
questions.explanation: Speed of sound in air at 20°C is about 343 m/s (approximately 1,235 km/h). Mach 1 = speed of sound. Aircraft flying faster than Mach 1 are supersonic.

questions.question: The human ear can hear sounds in the frequency range:
questions.explanation: The human audible range is 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. Sounds below 20 Hz are infrasound (heard by elephants and whales). Sounds above 20,000 Hz are ultrasound (heard by bats and dogs). Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz).

questions.question: SONAR (Sound Navigation And Ranging) works on the principle of:
questions.explanation: SONAR uses ultrasound waves emitted into water; they reflect off objects (submarines, fish, seabed) and return to the source. By measuring the time taken, distance is calculated. Used in submarines and fish finding.

questions.question: The 'kitchen of the cell' (food factory) is:
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are called the 'kitchen of the cell' because they carry out photosynthesis — using sunlight, CO₂, and water to produce glucose (food) and oxygen. Found only in plant cells. Contain chlorophyll.

questions.question: Nuclear fusion is the process of:
options: Combining light nuclei (e.g., hydrogen isotopes) to form heavier nuclei, releasing energy
questions.explanation: Nuclear fusion: light nuclei (H-2 deuterium + H-3 tritium) fuse at extremely high temperatures to form heavier nuclei (He-4), releasing enormous energy. Occurs in stars (the sun). Future clean energy source (ITER project).

questions.question: Einstein's famous equation E = mc² means:
options: Energy equals mass times the speed of light squared — mass and energy are equivalent
questions.explanation: E = mc² (Einstein, 1905): energy (E) = mass (m) × speed of light squared (c²). This shows mass and energy are equivalent. Small mass converts to enormous energy (nuclear reactions). Basis for nuclear technology.

questions.question: Lasers work on the principle of:
options: Stimulated emission of radiation — coherent, monochromatic, collimated light
questions.explanation: LASER (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation): photons stimulate excited atoms to emit identical photons — creating coherent (same phase), monochromatic (single wavelength), collimated (parallel) light. Used in medicine, cutting, communications.

questions.question: The green pigment in plants responsible for photosynthesis is:
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll (mainly chlorophyll-a and chlorophyll-b) absorbs red and blue light for photosynthesis. Carotene (orange) and xanthophyll (yellow) are accessory pigments. Anthocyanin gives red/purple color to flowers/fruits.

questions.question: Birds belong to the class:
questions.explanation: Birds belong to Class Aves. They are warm-blooded vertebrates with feathers, beaks, hollow bones (for flight), and lay eggs with hard shells. About 10,000 species exist. Adapted for flight (wings, streamlined body).

questions.question: Adrenaline (epinephrine) is produced by:
options: Adrenal medulla (inner part of adrenal gland)
questions.explanation: Adrenaline (epinephrine) is produced by the adrenal medulla (inner part of adrenal glands, above kidneys). It is the 'fight or flight' hormone — increases heart rate, blood pressure, and blood glucose during stress.

questions.question: The hormone responsible for 'fight or flight' response is:
questions.explanation: Adrenaline (epinephrine) triggers the fight-or-flight response — dilates pupils, increases heart rate, raises blood pressure, diverts blood to muscles, releases glucose from glycogen stores.

questions.question: Which phenomenon explains why the sky is blue?
options: Rayleigh scattering of sunlight by air molecules (shorter wavelengths scatter more)
questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering: air molecules scatter shorter wavelengths (blue/violet) much more than longer wavelengths (red). The sky appears blue because scattered blue light reaches our eyes from all directions. At sunset, light travels farther, and blue is scattered away, leaving red/orange.

questions.question: A mirage is caused by:
options: Total internal reflection of light in hot air layers near the ground
questions.explanation: Mirage is caused by TIR — hot air near the ground is less dense (lower refractive index) than cooler air above. Light from the sky bends back upward as it tries to enter the low-density layer, creating an illusion of water (the sky reflection).

questions.question: The phenomenon of splitting of white light into its component colours is called:
questions.explanation: Dispersion: white light splits into its component colours (VIBGYOR) when it passes through a prism because different colours have different refractive indices. Violet refracts most; red least.

questions.question: Scattering of light by particles larger than wavelength of light is:
questions.explanation: Tyndall Effect: scattering of light by colloidal particles (particle size ~ wavelength of light). Makes a beam of light visible in a colloid (like dust in a sunbeam through a window). Named after John Tyndall.

questions.question: The principle of optical fiber is:
options: Total Internal Reflection (TIR) of light through a glass/plastic fiber
questions.explanation: Optical fibers work by TIR — light traveling through a dense glass core hits the glass-cladding interface at angles beyond the critical angle and is totally reflected, staying in the fiber over long distances. Used in communications.

questions.question: Which mirror is used in the rear-view mirror of vehicles?
options: Convex mirror (gives a wider field of view)
questions.explanation: Convex mirrors are used as rear-view/wing mirrors because they produce an erect, diminished virtual image with a wide field of view. Concave mirrors are used as shaving/makeup mirrors and in searchlights.

questions.question: Which mirror is used as a reflector in headlights and searchlights?
options: Concave mirror (converges light into a parallel beam)
questions.explanation: Concave (converging) mirrors are used in headlights, searchlights, solar concentrators, and telescopes. When a light source is placed at the focal point, the reflected rays form a parallel (collimated) beam.

questions.question: The refractive index of a medium is the ratio of:
options: Speed of light in vacuum to speed of light in that medium (n = c/v)
Wavelength in vacuum to wavelength in medium
Frequency in medium to frequency in vacuum
Density of medium to density of vacuum
questions.explanation: Refractive index (n) = c/v (speed of light in vacuum / speed in medium). Higher n = slower light, more bending. Glass n ≈ 1.5, Water n ≈ 1.33, Diamond n ≈ 2.42 (hence diamond's high reflectance).

questions.question: The noble gases (Group 18) are unreactive because:
options: They have completely filled outermost electron shells (8 electrons — stable octet)
They are liquids at room temperature
questions.explanation: Noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) have completely filled outermost electron shells (He=2, others=8), making them extremely stable and unreactive. They are called 'inert gases'. Used in lighting (neon, argon).

questions.question: Ultrasound imaging uses:
options: High-frequency sound waves (>20,000 Hz) to image internal organs
questions.explanation: Ultrasound imaging (sonography) uses high-frequency sound waves. A transducer sends sound waves into the body; echoes returning from organs are converted into images. Safe (no radiation) — used for fetal imaging, abdominal organs.

questions.question: The nearest star to Earth (after the Sun) is:
questions.explanation: Proxima Centauri is the nearest star to Earth at 4.24 light years (about 40 trillion km). It is part of the Alpha Centauri triple star system. It has at least one exoplanet (Proxima b) in the habitable zone.

questions.question: A 'light year' is:
options: The distance light travels in one year (about 9.46 × 10¹² km)
The time for light to reach the Moon
The age of the universe in years
questions.explanation: A light year is the distance light travels in one year: approximately 9.46 trillion km (9.46 × 10¹² km). It is a unit of distance, not time. Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth.

questions.question: The Big Bang theory states that the universe began:
options: About 13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense state that rapidly expanded
Was created by a star explosion
questions.explanation: Big Bang Theory: the universe began about 13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense singularity that rapidly expanded (Big Bang). Evidence: cosmic microwave background radiation (CMB), redshift of galaxies, abundance of light elements.

questions.question: Microwaves are used in cooking because:
options: They cause water molecules in food to vibrate and generate heat through friction
questions.explanation: Microwave ovens use ~2.45 GHz microwaves that cause water molecules to rotate/vibrate, generating heat through molecular friction. This heats food from inside out. Microwaves only affect water-containing foods.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when acids react with active metals (like Zn, Fe, Mg)?
options: Hydrogen gas (H₂) — Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑
questions.explanation: When active metals react with dilute acids, hydrogen gas is produced: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑. This can be tested by holding a burning splint near the gas — it burns with a squeaky pop sound.

questions.question: The autonomic nervous system controls:
options: Involuntary functions like heartbeat, digestion, breathing, and glandular secretions
questions.explanation: The autonomic nervous system (ANS) controls involuntary body functions: heart rate, blood pressure, digestion, glandular activity, and respiration. It has two divisions: sympathetic (fight-or-flight) and parasympathetic (rest-and-digest).

questions.question: The hormone that regulates the sleep-wake cycle is:
options: Melatonin (secreted by the pineal gland — more at night, less during day)
questions.explanation: Melatonin is secreted by the pineal gland in the brain. Its production increases in darkness (night) and decreases in light (day), regulating the circadian rhythm (24-hour sleep-wake cycle). Jet lag disrupts melatonin production.

questions.question: The process by which plants lose water through their leaves is:
options: Transpiration — primarily through stomata; drives the ascent of xylem sap
questions.explanation: Transpiration is the evaporation of water from plant leaves (mainly through stomata). It creates a tension (negative pressure) in the xylem, pulling water upward from roots. Factors affecting transpiration: temperature, humidity, wind, light.

questions.question: Pollination by wind is called:
options: Anemophily — pollen grains are light and produced in large quantities (e.g., grasses, corn)
questions.explanation: Anemophily (wind pollination): pollen is lightweight, small, smooth, and produced abundantly. Examples: grasses, wheat, maize, oak. Entomophily (insect pollination): pollen is sticky, fragrant flowers. Zoophily includes bird (ornithophily) and bat pollination.

questions.question: The focal length of a concave mirror is:
options: Negative (by sign convention) — half of the radius of curvature (f = R/2), and concave mirrors can form real or virtual images
questions.explanation: For a concave mirror (converging): focal length f = R/2 (R = radius of curvature). By the New Cartesian sign convention, f is negative for concave mirrors. Uses: headlights, solar furnaces, dental/ENT mirrors, reflecting telescopes (real image for object beyond f).

questions.question: Optical fiber communication works on the principle of:
options: Total Internal Reflection (TIR) — light trapped inside the fiber travels long distances with minimal loss
Reflection at a glass-air interface below critical angle
questions.explanation: Optical fibers use TIR: light enters the dense glass core and hits the core-cladding boundary at angles greater than the critical angle, reflecting completely back into the core. Light travels zigzag inside the fiber from source to destination with very low signal loss.

questions.question: The power of a lens is measured in:
options: Dioptre (D) — power = 1/focal length in meters; convex lens has +ve power, concave lens has -ve power
questions.explanation: Power of a lens P = 1/f (meters). Unit: dioptre (D). Convex (converging) lens: positive power. Concave (diverging) lens: negative power. Spectacle prescriptions use dioptres. Two thin lenses in contact: P = P₁ + P₂.

questions.question: The Doppler Effect in sound refers to:
options: Change in apparent frequency of sound when source and observer are in relative motion (higher freq when approaching, lower when receding)
questions.explanation: Doppler Effect: when a source moves toward the observer, sound waves compress → higher frequency (higher pitch). When moving away → lower frequency. Application: radar speed guns, ultrasound (fetal heartbeat), astronomical red-shift/blue-shift of light.

questions.question: An echo is produced when sound is reflected by a surface that is at least:
options: 17 meters away (sound takes at least 0.1 s to return — minimum delay for human ear to distinguish echo from original sound)
questions.explanation: For an echo to be heard distinctly, the reflected sound must return at least 0.1 second after the original. Since sound travels at ~340 m/s, total distance = 340 × 0.1 = 34 m. So the reflecting surface must be at least 34/2 = 17 m away.

questions.question: Ultrasound is used in medical sonography because:
options: High-frequency sound (>20,000 Hz) reflects differently at tissue boundaries, creating images without radiation (safe for fetal imaging)
It passes through all tissues without reflection
questions.explanation: Ultrasound (1-20 MHz for medical use) reflects at interfaces between different tissue densities. Transducer emits pulses and detects echoes; computer reconstructs images. Safe (no ionizing radiation), used for fetal imaging, organ scanning, and detecting kidney stones.

questions.question: 5G technology compared to 4G offers:
options: Much higher speeds (up to 20 Gbps vs 100 Mbps), lower latency (<1ms vs ~50ms), and supports more connected devices (IoT)
Only better coverage in rural areas
questions.explanation: 5G (5th Generation): peak speed up to 20 Gbps (20× faster than 4G), latency <1ms (vs 50ms for 4G), capacity for 1 million devices/km² (for IoT). Uses mmWave (high frequency, short range) and Sub-6 GHz bands. Enables autonomous vehicles, telemedicine, smart cities.

questions.question: Stars generate energy through:
options: Nuclear fusion — hydrogen atoms fuse to form helium in the core, releasing energy (E=mc²) — same process as hydrogen bomb
questions.explanation: Stars like the Sun fuse hydrogen (H) to helium (He) in their cores: 4 ¹H → ⁴He + energy. The slight mass decrease (mass defect) converts to energy via E=mc². The Sun converts ~600 million tons of H to He every second, releasing energy as light and heat.

questions.question: LED (Light Emitting Diode) produces light through:
options: Electroluminescence — electrons recombining with holes in a semiconductor p-n junction release energy as photons
questions.explanation: LED: when forward-biased, electrons from n-type and holes from p-type recombine at the junction, releasing energy as photons. Color depends on semiconductor material (bandgap): GaAs (infrared), GaN (blue/white), GaP (green/red). LEDs use 80-90% less energy than incandescent bulbs.

questions.question: In a nuclear power plant, a moderator is used to:
options: Slow down fast neutrons to thermal (slow) neutrons that are more effective at causing fission in U-235
questions.explanation: Moderator slows fast fission neutrons (MeV) to thermal neutrons (~0.025 eV) which have higher fission cross-section with U-235. Common moderators: heavy water (D₂O, used in Indian PHWRs like Kalpakkam), light water (H₂O), graphite. Control rods (boron/cadmium) absorb neutrons to control reaction.

questions.question: The speed of sound in air at 0°C is approximately:
options: 331 m/s (increases by 0.6 m/s per °C rise — at 25°C ≈ 346 m/s)
questions.explanation: Speed of sound in dry air at 0°C ≈ 331 m/s; at 25°C ≈ 346 m/s. Speed increases with temperature and humidity. Speed in water ≈ 1480 m/s; in steel ≈ 5100 m/s. Sound is fastest in solids because molecules are more tightly packed.

questions.question: The loudness of sound is measured in:
options: Decibels (dB) — a logarithmic scale; 0 dB = threshold of hearing; 120 dB = pain threshold; normal conversation ≈ 60 dB
questions.explanation: Decibel (dB) scale: 0 dB (threshold of hearing), 30 dB (whisper), 60 dB (normal conversation), 85 dB (hearing damage starts with prolonged exposure), 120 dB (pain threshold), 140+ dB (immediate damage). Frequency (pitch) is measured in Hertz (Hz).

questions.question: Photosynthesis occurs in which part of the plant cell?
options: Chloroplast — specifically in the thylakoid membranes (light reactions) and stroma (dark reactions/Calvin cycle)
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the sites of photosynthesis. Light reactions occur in the thylakoid membranes (stacks of grana) — capture light energy, produce ATP and NADPH, release O₂. Calvin cycle (dark reactions) occurs in the stroma — fixes CO₂ into glucose.

questions.question: The photoelectric effect (which Einstein explained in 1905) shows that light behaves as:
options: Particles (photons) — light must reach a minimum frequency threshold to eject electrons, regardless of intensity
Only as particles all the time
questions.explanation: Einstein's 1905 Nobel Prize (1921): photons have energy E = hf (h = Planck's constant). Below the threshold frequency, no electrons are ejected regardless of light intensity. Above it, number of electrons ∝ intensity. This proves wave-particle duality of light.

questions.question: Quantum mechanics was developed mainly by:
options: Planck, Bohr, Heisenberg, Schrödinger, Dirac, Pauli — early 20th century (1900-1930)
questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics origins: Planck (1900, blackbody radiation, quantized energy), Einstein (1905, photoelectric effect), Bohr (1913, hydrogen atom model), Heisenberg (1927, uncertainty principle), Schrödinger (1926, wave equation), Dirac (1928, relativistic quantum mechanics).

questions.question: The process by which green plants prepare food using sunlight is called photosynthesis. Which gas is released as a byproduct?
options: Oxygen (O₂) — released from splitting of water molecules (photolysis) during light reactions
questions.explanation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. O₂ is released from water molecules split during light reactions (photolysis of water). This O₂ is the source of almost all atmospheric oxygen. CO₂ is fixed into glucose in the Calvin cycle (dark reactions).

questions.question: The human eye can see objects clearly because:
options: The eye lens focuses light on the retina — ciliary muscles change lens shape (accommodation) for near and far vision
questions.explanation: The eye works like a camera: light enters through cornea (main refracting surface), passes through iris (controls light amount) and lens (fine-focuses by changing shape — accommodation), and forms an inverted image on the retina. Rods (dim light, black/white) and cones (color, bright light) in retina convert light to nerve signals.

questions.question: The pH of blood is maintained in the range:
options: 7.35–7.45 (slightly alkaline) — deviation causes acidosis (<7.35) or alkalosis (>7.45), both life-threatening
questions.explanation: Blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45 (slightly alkaline). Buffer systems: bicarbonate buffer (HCO₃⁻/H₂CO₃), protein buffer (hemoglobin), and phosphate buffer. Acidosis (pH < 7.35): excess CO₂ or metabolic acid. Alkalosis (pH > 7.45): excess base. Outside 6.8-7.8 is fatal.

questions.question: Earthquakes are measured using a:
options: Seismograph (instrument) — and measured on Richter scale (logarithmic, each unit = 10× amplitude) or Moment Magnitude Scale (Mw, more accurate for large quakes)
questions.explanation: Seismograph/seismometer records ground vibrations from earthquakes. Richter scale (Charles Richter, 1935): logarithmic scale based on wave amplitude. Moment Magnitude Scale (Mw, now preferred): measures seismic moment (fault area × displacement × rigidity). Epicenter: surface point above earthquake focus.

questions.question: The Milky Way galaxy contains approximately:
options: 100-400 billion stars — our solar system is about 26,000 light years from the galactic center, and takes ~225 million years to orbit it
questions.explanation: Milky Way: barred spiral galaxy, 100,000-180,000 light years in diameter, containing 100-400 billion stars. Our Sun is located in the Orion Arm (minor spiral arm), ~26,000 light years from the galactic center (where there is a supermassive black hole, Sagittarius A*). Nearest galaxy: Andromeda (~2.5 million light years).

questions.question: Which part of the eye controls the amount of light entering?
options: Iris (the colored part) — circular and radial muscles change pupil size: dilates in dim light (parasympathetic), constricts in bright light (sympathetic)
questions.explanation: Iris: colored part of the eye (contains pigment melanin — more melanin = darker iris). Circular muscles (parasympathetic): contract → pupil dilates (dim light). Radial muscles (sympathetic): contract → pupil constricts (bright light). Pupil is the hole through which light enters.

questions.question: The chemical used in photography (traditional silver-based film) is:
options: Silver bromide (AgBr) or silver chloride (AgCl) — light-sensitive, reduces to black silver when exposed to light
questions.explanation: Traditional photographic film: silver halides (AgBr, AgCl) coated on film. When light hits AgBr, Ag⁺ is reduced to Ag (black) — forming a latent image. Developer (chemical reductor) amplifies this; fixer (sodium thiosulfate) removes unexposed AgBr. Digital cameras replaced film with CCD/CMOS sensors.

questions.question: Photosynthesis equation is:
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants use sunlight (chlorophyll) to convert CO₂ and water into glucose (food) and oxygen. Oxygen is the by-product — vital for life on Earth.

questions.question: Vitamin D deficiency causes:
questions.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency causes Rickets (soft/weak bones in children — bowed legs) and Osteomalacia (soft bones in adults). Vitamin D is synthesized by skin in sunlight. Sources: fish liver oil, eggs, fortified milk.

questions.question: Night blindness is caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness (inability to see in dim light) and xerophthalmia (dry eyes). Vitamin A is needed for rhodopsin production in rod cells of retina. Sources: carrots, green leafy vegetables, fish liver.

questions.question: The pH of human blood is approximately:
questions.explanation: Human blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35-7.45 (slightly alkaline). Acidosis (pH < 7.35) and alkalosis (pH > 7.45) are life-threatening conditions. The body uses buffers (bicarbonate, phosphate) to maintain blood pH.

questions.question: C.V. Raman won the Nobel Prize in Physics (1930) for:
options: Discovery of Raman Effect (inelastic scattering of photons by molecules)
questions.explanation: C.V. Raman (Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman) discovered the Raman Effect in 1928 — when light scatters from molecules, a small fraction changes wavelength due to energy exchange with molecular vibrations. He won Nobel Prize in 1930 — first Asian scientist to win it.

questions.question: A seismograph is used to measure:
questions.explanation: A seismograph (seismometer) detects and records seismic waves from earthquakes and other ground vibrations. The Richter scale (and moment magnitude scale) measures earthquake intensity. Charles Richter developed the Richter scale (1935).

questions.question: Which instrument is used to measure the wavelength of light?
questions.explanation: A spectrometer (or spectroscope) disperses light into its component wavelengths (spectrum). Used to identify elements (each has unique spectral lines — like a fingerprint) and measure wavelengths. Basis of spectroscopy.

questions.question: The Raman Effect (for which C.V. Raman won Nobel Prize in Physics 1930) involves:
options: Inelastic scattering of light by molecules — scattered light has different wavelength from incident light
questions.explanation: Raman Effect (1928): when monochromatic light passes through a transparent medium, some scattered light has different wavelengths (Raman scattering — inelastic). This reveals molecular composition. C.V. Raman won Nobel Prize 1930 — first Asian Nobel laureate in sciences.

questions.question: DRDO (Defence Research and Development Organisation) is associated with:
options: Defence technology R&D — missiles (Agni, Prithvi), radar, armaments, Tejas fighter aircraft
questions.explanation: DRDO (est. 1958, HQ: New Delhi) is India's defence R&D agency. Key achievements: Agni and Prithvi ballistic missiles, BrahMos supersonic cruise missile (India-Russia JV), Tejas light combat aircraft, Arjun tank, and electronic warfare systems.

questions.question: A person who cannot see distant objects clearly (near-sighted) has:
questions.explanation: Myopia (short-sightedness): eyeball is too long or lens too curved; distant objects focus in front of retina. Corrected with concave (diverging/negative power) lens. Hypermetropia (long-sightedness): near objects focus behind retina; corrected with convex lens.

questions.question: The phenomenon responsible for the blue color of the sky is:
options: Rayleigh Scattering — shorter wavelengths (blue, violet) scatter more by atmospheric molecules
questions.explanation: Rayleigh Scattering: atmospheric molecules (N₂, O₂) scatter shorter wavelengths (blue, violet) more than longer wavelengths (red, orange). Blue is scattered in all directions, making the sky appear blue. At sunrise/sunset, light travels through more atmosphere → sky appears red/orange.

questions.question: Which vitamin is synthesized by the human body when exposed to sunlight?
options: Vitamin D (cholecalciferol) — UV-B rays convert 7-dehydrocholesterol in skin to Vitamin D3
questions.explanation: UV-B radiation from sunlight converts 7-dehydrocholesterol (in skin) to Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol). It is then processed in liver and kidneys. Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption, bone health, and immune function. Deficiency causes rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults).

questions.question: The function of auxin (IAA) in plants is:
options: Promote cell elongation in stems (phototropism — shoot bends toward light because auxin redistributes to shaded side)
questions.explanation: Auxin (Indole-3-acetic acid, IAA) is produced in shoot tips. It promotes cell elongation. In phototropism, light causes auxin to move to the shaded side of the stem, causing more elongation there, bending the shoot toward light. High concentrations inhibit root growth.

questions.question: The National Institute of Ocean Technology (NIOT) is located in:
options: Chennai, Tamil Nadu — develops deep-sea mining technology, ocean thermal energy conversion (OTEC), and underwater vehicles
questions.explanation: NIOT (National Institute of Ocean Technology) is in Chennai (established 1993). It developed: desalination plants powered by wave energy, deep-sea submersibles (for Samudrayaan mission), OTEC technology, and undersea mining tools for polymetallic nodules.

questions.question: The hormone produced by the adrenal medulla during stress is:
options: Adrenaline (epinephrine) — triggers 'fight-or-flight' response: increased heart rate, blood pressure, blood glucose
questions.explanation: Adrenal medulla secretes adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline in response to sudden stress. Effects (fight-or-flight): increased heart rate and blood pressure, dilated pupils, bronchodilation, increased blood glucose, reduced digestive activity. Short-term acute response. Cortisol (adrenal cortex) handles long-term stress.

questions.question: The full form of LASER is:
options: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
Light Amplified by Solar Energy Radiation
questions.explanation: LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation. Principle: stimulated emission — an excited atom emits a photon identical to the incident photon when struck by another photon. This creates a coherent, monochromatic, collimated beam. Applications: barcode scanners, CD/DVD players, surgery, fiber optics, cutting, and holography.

questions.question: The phenomenon of diffraction of light proves that:
options: Light has wave nature — waves bend around obstacles and spread through narrow openings (slit width ≈ wavelength of light)
Light travels in straight lines always
questions.explanation: Diffraction: waves bend around obstacles or spread after passing through narrow openings (most pronounced when opening ≈ wavelength). Light shows diffraction (proving wave nature), but the effect is small because light wavelengths are tiny (400-700 nm). CD surface diffraction creates rainbow colors.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a greenhouse gas?
options: Oxygen (O₂) and Nitrogen (N₂) — they do not absorb infrared radiation
questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit infrared (thermal) radiation: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapor, ozone, CFCs. O₂ and N₂ (which make up ~99% of atmosphere) do NOT absorb IR radiation — they are NOT greenhouse gases. This is why the atmosphere is transparent to incoming sunlight but traps outgoing heat.

questions.question: The speed of light in vacuum is approximately:
options: 3 × 10⁸ m/s (300,000 km/s) — the fastest speed possible; light from Sun reaches Earth in ~8.3 minutes
questions.explanation: Speed of light in vacuum: c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s = 300,000 km/s. Light takes: 8.3 minutes from Sun to Earth, 1.3 seconds from Moon to Earth, 4.24 years from Proxima Centauri. Speed of light is reduced in media (glass, water) — basis of refraction.

questions.question: The 'Big Bang' theory explains:
options: The origin of the universe ~13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense singularity that rapidly expanded — supported by CMB radiation and redshift of galaxies
The formation of black holes only
questions.explanation: Big Bang theory (Georges Lemaître, 1927; confirmed by Hubble, Penzias & Wilson): ~13.8 billion years ago, the universe began from a singularity of infinite density/temperature and has been expanding ever since. Evidence: cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation (Penzias & Wilson, 1964 Nobel), redshift of galaxies (Hubble, 1929).

questions.question: The human ear can detect frequencies in the range of:
options: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz) — below 20 Hz is infrasound; above 20 kHz is ultrasound (bats, dolphins, dog whistles use ultrasound)
questions.explanation: Human audible range: 20 Hz (very deep bass) to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz, high-pitched). This narrows with age (presbycusis). Infrasound (<20 Hz): earthquakes, large animals, some whales. Ultrasound (>20 kHz): bats (echolocation), dolphins, medical imaging (1-20 MHz), dog whistles (~40 kHz).

questions.question: The layer of Earth's atmosphere where most weather phenomena occur is:
options: Troposphere (0-12 km altitude) — contains ~75% of atmospheric mass, all water vapor; temperature decreases with altitude
Stratosphere (12-50 km) — where ozone layer is
questions.explanation: Troposphere: lowest atmospheric layer (0-8 km at poles, 0-16 km at equator). Contains: 75% of atmospheric mass, all water vapor and clouds, all weather events. Temperature decreases with altitude at ~6.5°C/km. Stratosphere above: weather-free, ozone layer, jets fly here for smooth flight.
